prompt:
Theorem title: Weak formulation of the Poisson problem via Lax–Milgram

Ambient theorem statement (context only; do not formalize this whole statement unless it exactly matches the target node):
Weak formulation of the Poisson problem via Lax–Milgram.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded domain with sufficiently regular boundary, and let \(f \in L^2(\Omega)\). Then there exists a unique \(u \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) such that
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla v\,dx
=
\int_\Omega f v\,dx
\qquad \text{for all } v \in H_0^1(\Omega).
\]
Moreover,
\[
\|u\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)} \le C \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)}.
\]

Primary formalization target: the target node's informal statement and informal proof text below. Do not try to prove the ambient theorem statement itself unless it is identical to the target node.

Target theorem name: node_cont_ell_aristotle

Scratch file to rewrite: FormalIslands/Generated/node_cont_ell_worker_20260406-043049_5d538a00.lean

Target node:

- id: node_cont_ell
- title: Continuity of the linear functional $\ell$ via Poincaré
- informal statement:
The linear functional $\ell(v) := \int_\Omega f v\,dx$ is bounded on $H_0^1(\Omega)$; specifically, $|\ell(v)| \le C_P \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \|v\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)}$.
- informal proof text:
By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality,
$$|\ell(v)| = \left|\int_\Omega f v\,dx\right| \le \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\|v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}.$$
Applying the Poincaré inequality on $H_0^1(\Omega)$,
$$\|v\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \le C_P \|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)} = C_P \|v\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)},$$
we obtain $|\ell(v)| \le C_P \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \|v\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)}$, so $\ell \in (H_0^1(\Omega))^*$.
- formalization priority: 1
- formalization rationale:
This node chains two distinct analytic tools—Cauchy–Schwarz and the Poincaré inequality on $H_0^1(\Omega)$—into a concrete norm estimate. Both tools have Mathlib counterparts (`inner_mul_le_norm_mul_norm`, `Poincare_inequality` or `SobolevInequality`), but composing them correctly under the $H_0^1$ seminorm requires careful norm unfolding. It is the most technically delicate subclaim and the one most likely to surface definitional issues in a formal assistant.
